coredata.dc:description: This study aimed to produce bio-oils from various lignocellulosic biomass, namely palm empty fruit bunch (EFB), palm fiber (PF), sugarcane bagasse (SB), and sugarcane leaves (SL). Hydrothermal liquefaction, an environmentally benign process, was applied for converting wet bio-resources to bio-oils. The effect of Na2CO3 and La2O3 catalysts were investigated at 300 °C, the results showed higher bio-oil yield from Na2CO3 compared to La2O3. The use of glycerol solvent as a hydrogen donor substantially enhanced bio-oil yield for 300% compared to aqueous solvent. Furthermore, the microwave-assisted wet torrefaction (800 W for 20 min) pretreatment employed for EFB helps to increase aliphatic and aromatic compounds and enhance the light and heavy bio-oils yields to 76.80 wt%. SB and EFB are promising biomass for solvothermolysis liquefaction giving the highest energy ratio. A substantial amount of carbon nanodots and nanoflakes were produced from hydrothermolysis and solvothermolysis liquefaction of EFB with average mean diameters of 49.5 and 100.4 nm, respectively.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aimed to produce bio-oils from various lignocellulosic biomass, namely palm empty fruit bunch (EFB), palm fiber (PF), sugarcane bagasse (SB), and sugarcane leaves (SL). Hydrothermal liquefaction, an environmentally benign process, was applied for converting wet bio-resources to bio-oils. The effect of Na2CO3 and La2O3 catalysts were investigated at 300 °C, the results showed higher bio-oil yield from Na2CO3 compared to La2O3. The use of glycerol solvent as a hydrogen donor substantially enhanced bio-oil yield for 300% compared to aqueous solvent. Furthermore, the microwave-assisted wet torrefaction (800 W for 20 min) pretreatment employed for EFB helps to increase aliphatic and aromatic compounds and enhance the light and heavy bio-oils yields to 76.80 wt%. SB and EFB are promising biomass for solvothermolysis liquefaction giving the highest energy ratio. A substantial amount of carbon nanodots and nanoflakes were produced from hydrothermolysis and solvothermolysis liquefaction of EFB with average mean diameters of 49.5 and 100.4 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, the circular economy concept has been dominating the research and development toward the utilization of renewable resources in an effective way. In Thailand, biomass from agro-industrial processing, mainly, oil palm wastes and sugarcane wastes, is abundant. Conventionally, burning this lignocellulosic biomass is the easiest way to manage them, however it pollutes the atmosphere with toxic gases and particulate matters with low energy efficiency derived. Therefore, cleaner and eco-friendly technology for biomass conversion should be utilized to solve this situation. Various thermochemical technologies have been developed for transforming biomass to energy and fuels, such as fast pyrolysis [1], catalytic pyrolysis [2], gasification [3], and hydrothermal liquefaction [4]. Among the thermochemical conversion of biomass to bio-crude, hydrothermal liquefaction (HTL) has been considered as green, and environmentally friendly process, since all the carbon particulate, gaseous products, and liquid fuels are kept in the closed system.

Bio-crude oil is a highly viscous liquid, which is a major product from liquefaction of biomass, especially lignocellulosic and algal biomass [5]. Like petroleum crude oil, it can be used as a fuel or building block chemical directly or after a post-treatment such as deoxygenation, hydrogenation, hydrodeoxygenation, and purification [6]. Apart from environmental benign advantages of liquefaction by hydro- or solvothermolysis, a significantly lower oxygen-to-carbon molar ratio (O/C) from liquefaction (0.4–0.5) [7] has been reported relative to that from pyrolysis which is usually around 0.7–0.8 [8]. An increase of carbon content in bio-crude makes the heating value of bio-crude from hydro- or solvothermolysis liquefaction greater than that from pyrolysis.

Among thermal liquefaction conversion methods, solvothermolysis is the most extensively used regarding its relatively lower operating temperature when compared with pyrolysis. Additionally, solvothermolysis assists to prevent the formation of tar owing to the cross-linked reaction [9]. In general, biomass liquefaction through solvothermolysis occurs at moderate conditions (100–200 °C) in the presence of base or acid catalysts, and the solvents such as ethanol, acetone, ethylene glycol, polyethylene glycol, phenol, tetralin, n-dodecane have been intensively investigated [7,10,11]. Alternatively, the use of hydrogen-donor solvents for biomass liquefaction has increasingly gained attention [12]. Hydrogen-donor solvents mainly donate hydrogen atoms and improve the transport mechanism of hydrogen toward biomass structure to boost up liquid oil yield with oxygen reduction. Moreover, relative to non-renewable solvents such as polyhydric alcohols, the use of glycerol is taken into account as it is a renewable and sustainable bio-solvent derived byproducts from biodiesel production. In the case of catalyst for hydrothermal liquefaction, potassium carbonate has been found as a promising catalyst to transform waste bamboo chopsticks to bio-oil in a temperature range of 290–380 °C [13]. Similar research also demonstrated the positive influence of carbonates, chlorides, oxides, and sulfates of metals on delignification, biomass conversion, and liquid bio-crude yields in both hydro- and solvothermolysis [14,15]. Furthermore, it has been reported that wet torrefaction using either acid-catalyzed or hydrothermal treatment for the improvement of fixed carbon by reduction of volatile and an ash content of hydrochar from torrefaction [16]. An enhancement of bio-oil yield from biomass liquefaction was achieved in shorter reaction time when treating biomass with microwave irradiation [17,18]. Removal of acetyl groups from hemicellulose during microwave-assisted acid pretreatment/hydrothermal irradiation was accomplished, and thus acetic acid and acetyl groups containing products are reduced [19].

By far, most of biomass liquefaction studies focus on the enhancement of bio-oil yield and heating value. This work aimed to elucidate bio-oil composition and analyze the correlation between energy yield and energy ratio of biomass transformation based on heating value of raw materials. Palm empty fruit bunch (EFB), palm fiber (PF), sugarcane leaves (SL) and sugarcane bagasse (SB) were the raw materials for biomass liquefaction. Effect of glycerol as a hydrogen-donor solvent compared with water, and the effect of alkali in the form of carbonate and oxide catalysts were additionally studied.
In this study, the influence of microwave-assisted wet-torrefaction on increasing yield, energy ratio, and carbon content of bio-crude has been disclosed.

Sugarcane bagasse was obtained from Khonburi Sugar Public Company Limited, Thailand. Sugarcane leaves was supplied from the agricultural field in Sukhothai Province, Thailand. Palm empty fruit bunch (the leftover part after palm fruits are removed from fruit bunches) and palm fiber (palm mesocarp fiber also known as palm pressed fiber, which is the residue obtained after pressing palm fruit for oil extraction) were obtained from Chumporn Palm Oil Industry Public Company Limited, Thailand. Glycerol and sodium carbonate (Ajax Finechem), toluene (RCI Labscan), lanthanum oxide (Alfa Aesar) were used as received without modification. All of the chemicals used in this work were analytical grade.

The SB, SL, EFB, and PF samples were initially washed with distilled water and dried in an oven at 105 °C for 12 h. Dry raw materials were chopped using a 17-inch Guillotine paper cutter to 1–2 cm. The dry samples were milled using a centrifugal milling machine (three-HP custom-made model with 1-mm screen) and subsequently sieved using screen 40, 50, 100, and 200 mesh for 5 min with vibratory sieve shaker (AS300 Control, Retsch, Germany). The sieved biomass with a consistent particle size smaller than mesh 50, and larger than mesh 200 was collected and used for liquefaction to control the repeatability of reaction. The moisture content of the feedstock was then determined by moisture analyzer (Ohaus MB120, USA) for dry mass calculation. Then the samples with moisture content of ∼5–6 wt% were kept in a desiccator for further use.

The hydrothermal liquefaction (HTL) was conducted in a 500-mL custom-made reactor (stainless steel 316L, max tempaterature of 500 °C, max pressure of 20 MPa). In the experiment, 10 g of biomass (dry weight), 100 mL of deionized water and 1 g of catalyst (Na2CO3 or La2O3) were mixed in the reactor. The reactor cover was closed tightly using graphite gasket to prevent the gas leak. The reactants were agitated at 150 rpm using a U-shape stirrer equipped with a magnetic seal drive. Nitrogen gas was supplied to set the initial pressure at 8 MPa, and the temperature was increased to 300 °C with heating rate 10 °C min−1. The operating pressure during liquefaction was controlled using vent valve equipped with pressure transducer. The released gas entered the condenser unit, as shown in Fig. 1 and condensed to a liquid called condensed bio-oil (CBO). After 1 h reaction, the reactor cooled down to room temperature by ice bath with refrigerated circulator at 0 °C (NESLAB, NH03801, USA) through a U-shape tube inserted into the reactor. The remaining gas in the system was vent and gas compositional analysis was conducted for the optimal condition. Bio-crude oil (BCO) and liquid which was light bio-oil (LBO) products were separated by separation funnel, weighed and measured the volume for yield calculation. High heating value (HHV) and ultimate analysis were measured using bomb calorimeter (PARR 1261) and CHNS/O analyzer (Thermo Scientific™ FLASH 2000), respectively. For Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (GC-MS) analysis, liquid-liquid extraction using toluene as an extractant was carried out, and the extracted phase was collected and analyzed for the identification of the products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of catalyst and solvent on biomass liquefaction
ce:section.ce:para: To study the effect of catalyst on biomass hydrothermolysis and solvothermolysis, addition of 10 wt% Na2CO3 and La2O3 based on biomass weight was conducted, and the results were compared with the non-catalytic system. The influence of two solvents, water and glycerol, was also studied, using 100 mL of each solvent mixed with 10 g dry-weight biomass and 1 g catalyst. The BCO derived from biomass liquefaction could be separated into two fractions, i.e., LBO from the top layer and heavy bio-oil (HBO) from the bottom layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of microwave-assisted wet torrefaction on biomass liquefaction
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of microwave-assisted wet torrefaction on bio-crude and bio-oil quantity and quality was investigated. Before biomass liquefaction, 2 g dry-weight biomass was mixed with 20 mL solvent (water or glycerol) and subjected to microwave irradiation (Multiwave PRO 8NXF100, Anton Paar, Austria) at different power of 400, 600 and 800 W for 20 min with a 2 min heating up duration. Five vessels of microwave chambers were used simultaneously for the total 10 g biomass and 100 mL solvent. After microwave torrefaction, all slurry from five microwave reaction vessels were collected and mixed with 1 g catalyst (10 wt% of biomass weight). After 1 h hydrothermal liquefaction of the microwave-assisted torrefied slurry, bio-crude and bio-oil yield, HHV and chemical composition analyzed by GC-MS.

For liquefaction of biomass using water as a solvent, bio-oil was extracted from condensed liquid and liquid-phase products using toluene. The volume of condensed liquid or liquid-phase products was measured and recorded before mixing with 15 mL toluene. After vigorously stirring at 1000 rpm for 15 min, the solution is left in a separatory funnel for the separation. Bio-oil collected from toluene extractant at the top of the layer was used for yield, compositional and HHV analysis.

In the case of bio-oil extraction from bio-crude when glycerol was used as a solvent in liquefaction, the volume of LBO and HBO derived from liquefaction was measured. Subsequently, the whole volume of light bio-oil was mixed in 10 mL toluene. In the case of heavy bio-oil, 1 g HBO and 20 mL toluene were mixed, and the solution was stirred at 1000 rpm for 15 min and left in a separatory funnel for 30 min, as shown in Fig. 2 . Extracted light bio-oil and heavy bio-oil was collected on the top of the toluene layer for GC-MS analysis.

Elemental analysis of raw material and bio-oil was performed using CHNS/O analyzer (Thermo Scientific™ FLASH 2000). For GC-MS analysis of bio-oil, 2 μL of sample was injected with the split ratio of 10/1 into a Gas Chromatography (Agilent Technologies 6890N) equipped with HP-5MS UI capillary column (Agilent, 30 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 μm). Helium (He) was the carrier gas with a flow rate of 1 mL min−1, and the range of mass to charge ratio (m/z) was between 30 and 400. The injector temperature was 300 °C, and the interface temperature was 280 °C. The detector temperature was 250 °C, and the GC oven temperature was adjusted to three steps: 1) starting temperature (50 °C) was held for 2 min, 2) increasing temperature with ramp rate of 8 °C min−1 to 290 °C, and 3) holding temperature at 290 °C for 5 min.

The HHV of bio-oil was determined by using a bomb calorimeter (PARR 1261, USA). The standard heat combustion was set up using 1 g benzoic acid, and the heat of combustion was determined by burning 1.0 ± 0.2 g of the bio-oil sample in the bomb calorimeter. The heat of combustion was calculated based on temperature observed before and after combustion.

For more effective analysis of the optimal condition for liquefaction of biomass, the energy ratio, which is the factor that exhibits an enhanced HHV of bio-oil based on HHV of raw material is assessed. The energy ratio (r) of the product was calculated based on the difference between HHV of bio-crude or bio-oil (HHVbc, MJ kg−1) obtained and HHV of initial biomass (HHV0, MJ kg−1) as shown in eq. (5). Moreover, the energy yield (YE) that includes both HHV and bio-oil yield based on raw material, could be efficiently used as a screening factor for selecting a promising liquefaction process, can be calculated according to eq. (6) when Ybc is the mass yield of bio-crude or bio-oil.

Vent gas from liquefaction was analyzed by gas chromatography-thermal conductivity detector (GC-TCD). For analysis of CO, CO2, and CH4, a GC-TCD (Shimadzu Corporation GC-8A, Japan) with active carbon 608 column (2 m × 3 mm) was used. Helium (He) was the carrier gas with a flow rate of 40 mL min−1. The injector temperature and the detector temperature were 120 °C. Further, the column temperature was controlled at 100 °C. For analysis of CH4 and H2, a Shimadzu gas chromatograph GC-2014, Japan with molecular sieve 13X column was used. Argon (Ar) was the carrier gas with a flow rate of 35 mL min−1. The injector temperature was 80 °C. Further, the column temperature was controlled at 60 °C. The composition of gaseous products was calculated on a mole basis.

For assessment of the morphology of carbon particle generated from biomass liquefaction, the toluene extracted bio-oil was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min at 20 °C (Eppendorf Centrifuge 5804 R, USA). The supernatant was taken for the analysis using Transmission Electron Microscope [21] (Philips EM 420 TEM) with 120 kV electron beam.

Proximate analysis of raw materials i.e., EFB, PF, SB, and SL showed a good relationship between chemical composition and HHV of raw materials as demonstrated in Table 1 . PF contained the highest lignin content and thus gave the highest HHV value due to greater combustion efficiency. However, it has been reported that cellulose content plays a vital role in pyrolysis and liquefaction. The biomass contained high cellulose content dominates to achieve high yield and high quality of bio-oil via thermochemical conversion. A previous work reported that fast pyrolysis of cellulose achieved up to 84 wt% bio-oil yield at 500 °C while lignin and xylan gave only 55.4% at pyrolysis temperature of 550 °C and 500 °C, respectively [22]. An increase in temperature produced more oxygenated compounds in bio-oil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of catalyst on hydrothermal liquefaction of EFB, PF, SB and SL
ce:section.ce:para: Effect of lanthanum oxide (La2O3) and sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) on bio-oil and bio-char yield of biomass hydrothermal liquefaction was investigated. As demonstrated in Table 2 , the yields of bio-oil from EFB for all runs were substantially higher than those from other biomass (PF, SB, and SL). Na2CO3 catalyst provided the highest bio-oil yield of 18.55 wt% from EFB hydrothermal liquefaction. However, when compared with LBO yields from previous work [14,23–26] as shown in Table 2, our LBO yields were lower. This was possibly owing to the substantially lower pressure (8 MPa) was used in the present study relative to other works (20–40 MPa), therefore, the biomass structure could not be completely broken down and greater bio-char yield was obtained instead of bio-oil. The addition of Na2CO3 catalyst significantly enhanced the bio-oil yield from hydrothermal liquefaction of EFB, SB, and SL relative to La2O3 and without a catalyst. Alkali salts such as potassium carbonate (K2CO3) and sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) have been reported to improve the hydrolysis of cellulose and hemicellulose macromolecules to small fragments [27,28]. Further, these small fragments subsequently react and form other building block chemicals and bio-oil categorized as ketones, phenolics, aromatics, as shown in Fig. 3 .

Moreover, higher HHV values were achieved from bio-oil and bio-char compared with HHVs of raw EFB, PF, and SL. From Table 2, HHV of LBO was also higher than that of CBO. The high HHV value of LBO (45.04 MJ kg−1) was obtained from SL liquefaction with La2O3, and the HHV of LBO from EFB liquefaction was 41.24 MJ kg−1 from the liquefaction without a catalyst. However, when HHVs was calculated based on bio-oil yield, hydrothermal liquefaction of EFB using Na2CO3 catalyst exhibited the highest HHV value. Moreover, when total HHV of all products (bio-oil + bio-char) was taken into consideration, the energy ratio of EFB, PF, and SL was greater than 1.0, suggesting that liquefaction process is practicable regarding the energy recovery. However, SB gave the lowest total HHV and energy ratio as shown in Table 2. Although the energy ratio of SB from hydrothermal liquefaction of the present work (Energy ratio 0.74–0.87) was comparable with previous works (Energy ratio 0.26–0.86), the results suggested that the combustion of raw SB was more feasible for energy production than hydrothermal liquefaction of SB.

For the chemical compositional analysis of both CBO and LBO by GC-MS, phenolic group was mainly contained in both CBO and LBO as depicted in Fig. 3(A) and (B). When considering the chemical structure of lignin, which is a very complex polyphenolic structure [29], it can be converted to benzene, toluene, xylene, and phenol in the form of coumaryl alcohol [30]. From Fig. 3(A) and (B), phenolic derivatives were the principal compounds found in CBO and LBO from catalytic hydrothermal liquefaction of EFB, PF, SB and SL when Na2CO3 was used as the catalyst. It was postulated that after delignification process catalyzed by alkali salt, phenylpropane units which are the basic structure in lignin, are thereby dissolved and decomposed to phenolic compounds [31]. In the present study, phenol was the main constituent in LBO for both La2O3 and Na2CO3 catalysts. Na2CO3 catalyst showed 2-time higher phenol content compared with La2O3 with the %area more than 40% for SB and >30% for EFB and SL, and >20% for PF. Both catalysts also contributed substantially high yield of 2-methoxy-phenol over 10% area for EFB, PF and SB, and over 20% area for SL. Phenol derivatives, namely 4-ethyl phenol (4.7–8.8%), 4-methyl phenol (2.5–3.3%), and 4-ethyl-2-methoxy- Phenol (1.1–2.0%) were produced in LBO for both catalysts in a respective degree.

For the chemical composition of LBO derived from EFB liquefaction (Fig. 3(A)), it contained a higher amount of aromatic compounds compared with other raw materials. Major aromatic compounds observed in bio-oil of EFB liquefaction by both catalysts were 2,3-dimethyl-2-cyclopenten-1-one (4.0–5.2%), and 3,4-dimethyl-2-cyclopenten-1-one (1.2–5.3%), however, Na2CO3 catalyzed liquefaction provided higher yield relative to La2O3 and the control without catalyst. It has been reported that 1, 2-cyclopentanedione was generated simultaneously with carbon dioxide (CO2) and hydrogen (H2) from the thermochemical conversion of 2,3-dihydro-3,5-dihydroxy-6-methyl-4H-Pyran-4-one (DHMDHP) during thermochemical break down of cellulose [22]. The extra hydrogen generated can be utilized in-situ and contribute for other product formation such as conversion of 1,2-cyclopentanedione to 2,3-dimethyl-2-cyclopenten-1-one via hydrogenation.

Among aromatic compounds produced, 2,5-dimethyl-2,5-cyclohexadiene-1,4-dione was found when using Na2CO3 catalyst, and 2,5-dimethyl-2-cyclopentenone was detected in the presence of La2O3, suggesting that the bio-oil has comparable quality similar to aromatic hydrocarbon content in the conventional fuel [32]. In summary, in terms of greater energy ratio, bio-oil yield, and HHV of bio-oil derived from EFB, Na2CO3 was the most suitable catalyst for biomass liquefaction for further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of glycerol on liquefaction of EFB, PF, SB and SL
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of glycerol in solvothermolysis liquefaction on the bio-oil yield, composition, and HHV was investigated by using the glycerol-to-biomass ratio of 12.6:1 and 10 wt% Na2CO3 based on biomass weight. After liquefaction, a clear separation of LBO and HBO was obtained, as shown in Fig. 2. The results demonstrated that extremely high bio-oil yield from EFB solvothermolysis liquefaction using glycerol was achieved (57.24 wt%; Table 3 ). Moreover, EFB and SL exhibited high LBO + HBO yield of 74.02 and 74.06 wt%, respectively, compared with that from PF and SB which yielded 67.37 and 64.15 wt% as demonstrated in Fig. S1. It has been revealed that glycerol contributes to an increase in the hydrolysis rate of a macromolecule of lignocellulose into a smaller fragment that can increase the yield of bio-oil. Compared with other previous studies, the use of glycerol in liquefaction exhibited higher HHVs and carbon yield in the present work. However, the bio-oil yield was less than other reported studies [33–35], as demonstrated in Table 3. Therefore, the greater energy ratio calculated based on yield, and higher HHVs was obtained in our study.

According to the chemical composition analysis of the bio-oil by GC-MS (Table S1 and Table S2), using glycerol as a solvent instead of water resulted in alteration of composition ratio in both LBO and HBO. Fig. 4 (A) and (B) also showed that phenol and its derivatives were significantly decreased from 70% when liquefaction in water to 20–30% in LBO and 25–38% in HBO when liquefaction in glycerol, while ketone and aromatics increased substantially. The results were contradictory to a previous study on liquefaction of rice straw in glycerol catalyzed by alkali salt [33], which revealed that liquefaction at a lower temperature (260 °C, initial oxygen pressure 1 MPa, 1 h) led to the formation of ketones rather than a carboxylic acid. Moreover, a previous work reported that glycerol and Na2CO3 were favorable for the formation of phenolic compounds that could be derived from the decomposition of phenylpropane units of lignin structure [34]. However, the present study shows that phenolic compounds were greatly decreased, while ketone, aromatic and aliphatic compounds were markedly enhanced using glycerol as a solvent. The different results were possibly due to the dissimilar atmospheric gas during liquefaction between nitrogen (the present study) and oxygen (the previous study). The presence of oxygen leads to generate phenol oxygenates or acid instead of aromatics and ketone. Furthermore, silica which is a major element contained in rice straw may act as an acidic catalyst, which is favorable for the oxidation of lignocellulose results in the degradation products under oxygen atmosphere.

For the composition of LBO and HBO, as shown in Tables S1 and S2, aromatic and aliphatic compositions was found. Among the aromatics in LBO, 3-methyl-2-cyclopenten-1-one, 2,3-dimethyl-2-cyclopenten-1-one, 1,2-dimethyl-benzene, 1,3-dimethyl-benzene, 1,4-dimethyl-benzene, 1-ethyl-2-methyl-benzene, and 2,3-dihydro-1,4-benzodioxin-2-methanol were mainly detected. In HBO, a similar composition was observed with a different quantity. Aliphatic compounds mostly found in the study were 2,5-hexanedione, 2,4-hexadienal, and 9-octadecenamide.

The highest HHV value of LBO was obtained from EFB at 42.55 MJ kg−1, however, the different HHVs of LBO from PF, SB and SL were detected at 41.54, 40.15 and 41.34 MJ kg−1, respectively, as demonstrated in Fig. 4(C). In the case of HBO, HHVs of bio-oil from PF and SB were very similar at 41.07 and 41.01 MJ kg−1, respectively, followed by SL and EFB with HHV values of 40.01 and 37.77 MJ kg−1. Overall HHV values of the HBO and LBO from biomass were not significantly different because most of constituents were similar, i.e., ketone, aliphatic, phenolic, and aromatic groups. Then the comparison of energy ratio based on HBO and LBO yields was more suitable. As demonstrated in Table 3, EFB liquefaction in Na2CO3/glycerol system provided the highest energy ratio at 2.36 over the HHV of raw EFB. Liquefaction of PF, SB, and SL gave the energy ratio of 1.94, 2.12 and 2.32, respectively. Therefore, EFB was chosen to study the effect of microwave-assisted torrefaction on solvothermolysis liquefaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of microwave-assisted wet torrefaction on liquefaction of EFB in Na2CO3/glycerol system
ce:section.ce:para: In this part, solely EFB was treated by microwave pretreatment at 400, 600 and 800 W for 20 min in glycerol before liquefaction using Na2CO3 as the catalyst. It was found that the product after liquefaction in Na2CO3/glycerol system was bi-phasic liquid containing LBO and HBO. The top layer was a clear yellow (light bio-oil), and the lower layer was a black viscous liquid (heavy bio-oil). When microwave-assisted torrefaction was applied, the color of LBO was brighter and clearer regarding the depletion of fine char or particles than that of liquefaction without torrefaction.

The bio-oil yields (LBO + HBO) from EFB with microwave pretreatment at 400, 600 and 800W were 74.33, 74.86 and 75.06%, respectively. Although, total LBO + HBO yield was not different among all microwave power tested as well as the control experiment without pretreatment, microwave-assisted torrefaction of EFB at 600 W provided the high LBO yield, which is favorable biofuel for an internal combustion engine. It has been reported on wet torrefaction using microwave irradiation [36,37] since microwaves possess the ability to penetrate in the lignocellulosic material and the solution, thus accomplishing fast and volumetric heating [16,38]. The optimal microwave radiation energy enhances lignocellulose structural changes by a decrease in the degree of cellulose polymerization and crystallinity, increasing the specific surface [36], and thus accelerating the reduction of oxygenated compounds in biomass [39], therefore, carbon density should be enhanced, and the liquefaction efficiency could be improved.

Effect of microwave power on chemical composition of LBO and HBO is illustrated in Fig. 5 (A) and (B). When compared with LBO of untreated EFB, microwave pretreatment at 600 W was the most promising condition since the amount of aliphatics, which has long-chain hydrocarbon structure, together with phenolic compounds were enhanced. In the case of HBO, a significant increase of aromatic and aliphatic compounds was obtained from 600 W microwave torrefaction. In terms of the chemical composition of HBO, phenol, 2-methyl-phenol, 3-methyl-phenol, 2,5-dimethyl-phenol, and 2,6-dimethyl-phenol were found in a higher amount. Besides, aromatic compounds, 2-acetyl furan and 1-nitro cyclohexene, were most abundant constituents in bio-oil from EFB when microwave torrefaction was applied. This was presumably because deacetylation of hemicellulose occurred from microwave hydrothermal pretreatment compared with conventional hydrothermal pretreatment; which was attributed to the hot spot effect of microwave irradiation and a similar study on removal of acetyl groups of hemicellulose was found [16,19]. Although HHV values from all microwave powers are not significantly different (Fig. 5(C)), the highest energy ratio (2.38) based on yield was achieved from microwave-assisted wet torrefaction of EFB in glycerol at 400 W for 20 min as demonstrated in Table 3.

During torrefaction, biomass structure and chemical composition are modified by reduction or dehydration reaction. Therefore, the oxygen-to-carbon ratio was reduced and led to the increased energy content of biomass after torrefaction [40]. Various reports revealed that coal-like solid fuel and an enhanced fixed carbon content could be produced from biomass torrefaction [16,41]. In the present work, the carbon yield of LBO from microwave torrefaction before liquefaction was found to be enhanced for EFB when the microwave powers were 600 and 800 W. Maximum carbon yield for EFB was enhanced from 79.01 to 89.82% when treating at 600 W for 20 min. An increase of microwave power from 600 W to 800 W slightly lowered carbon yield (from 89.82% to 87.55%) due to excessive cleavage of lignocellulosic constituents to a smaller fraction which led to conversion to synthesis gas instead of bio-oil, resulting in lower LBO yield as shown in Table 3. Nevertheless, the total LBO and HBO yields from EFB pretreatment at 800 W for 20 min were highest among all powers tested. Thus, this torrefaction condition was selected for further investigation of all raw materials.

Analysis of vent gas (24.94 wt%) from solvothermal liquefaction after 800 W microwave torrefaction of EFB showed that the majority of gas composition was CO2 (32.11 mol%), while H2 (3.25 mol%), CO (2.96 mol%), CH4 (1.65%mol), was found in smaller amount and the rest was nitrogen gas from the pressurized N2 liquefaction system. The results were in good accordance with gaseous products from hydrogenolysis and gasification of glycerol over base catalysts that were found to be mostly hydrogen, and subsequently CO2, CH4, C2+, and a trace amount of CO, respectively [42]. When the gas products were solely considered without nitrogen, 80.35% CO2, 8.13% H2, 7.40% CO, and 4.13% CH4 were obtained, which were in good agreement with a previous work on biomass liquefaction that reported the vent gas composition of 94.2% CO2, 4.7% CO, 1.0% CH4, and 0.1% H2 [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave-assisted torrefaction for EFB, PF, SB, and SL liquefaction
ce:section.ce:para: In the study of the effect of microwave pretreatment at 800 W for 20 min in glycerol on the bio-oil yield and quality of all biomass was studied. From Table 3, it was found that the yields of bio-oil, for both HBO and LBO from all raw materials were slightly different. EFB treated with microwave at 800 W had the highest percentage of HBO + LBO (76.80%). Although the energy ratios of bio-oil were similar for EFB, PF, and SL, in contrast, energy ratio, as well as the bio-oil yield of SB, was extensively enhanced after pretreating with 800 W microwave irradiation. The electromagnetic energy in the microwave region was found to facilitate the elimination of oxygen atoms from the biomass by reducing chemical compounds in lignocellulose structure in the absence of water [44]. Microwave-assisted wet torrefaction of EFB and SB thus offered significant enhancement of carbon yield, which led to an increase of HHV and energy ratio.

Chemical composition in LBO and HBO could be categorized into five groups: phenolic, ketone, aromatic, aliphatic and acids as shown in Fig. 6 (A) and (B). The percentage of aromatic and aliphatic found in both LBO and HBO from EFB had the highest values, and they contained in SB in a respective degree. This occurrence reflected the HHV value of LBO + HBO as well as energy ratio, as demonstrated in Table 3. Therefore, microwave torrefaction at an optimal condition provided a beneficial effect on both the quantity and quality of bio-oil from biomass liquefaction.

The identification of compounds in LBO and HBO from all biomass was carried out by GC-MS. A similar composition of chemicals was obtained when compared with the liquefaction without torrefaction. However, for phenolic compounds, phenol, 2-methyl-phenol, 3-methyl-phenol, and 4-methyl-phenol were present in a higher amount in LBO when treated with the microwave. In the case of ketone, 2-methyl-2-cyclopentene-1, 3-methyl-2-cyclopentene-1, and 2,3-dimethyl-2-cyclopentene-1 were enhanced significantly compared with LBO without microwave pretreatment. The reason was mainly due to the demethylation reaction via hydrogen abstraction to phenol [45]. As the bond dissociation energy of O–CH3 is lower (280 kJ mol−1) than that of CH2–CH2 (ca. 370 kJ mol−1) [46], the demethylation could be promoted at low temperatures (<600 °C), while the latter occurs at high temperatures (>600 °C). Nevertheless, proper catalyst generates methyl radicals, and hence, methylation reaction occurs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the energy ratio and carbon yield of biomass liquefaction
ce:section.ce:para: To compare the efficiency of the liquefaction process, most important characteristics of bio-oil are yield and energy ratio. To achieve the high bio-crude (LBO + HBO) yield with high HHVs is the target of improvement of the process performance. However, the quality of generated bio-oil, which is associated with the high heating value itself, is an additional criterion to consider. A higher HHV of the oil implies that the higher portion of the energy of the feed that turns into the bio-crude and the lower share that turns into other products, i.e., char, gases, and water-soluble organics. This means that a greater part of oxygen in feed stock has been converted to other products such as gases and water soluble products during liquefaction, and thus lower bio-oil yield is attained [47]. The energy ratio and energy yield provide a complete assessment of the fuel processing in delivering energy to the bio-crude superior than the mere mass yield. Consequently, in this section, the energy ratio and energy yield, calculated based on mass yield and HHV of bio-crude relative to HHV of raw material, have been taken into consideration for the selection of process. Recently, the research on bio-oil production from the thermochemical conversion of biomass used various terms, such as energy ratio, energy yield, or energy recovery for comparing various processes [35,47–51].

Compared with hydrothermolysis liquefaction in water, a substantial enhancement of both energy yield and energy ratio was attained using solvothermolysis liquefaction in glycerol using Na2CO3 catalyst for EFB, PF, SB, and SL raw materials as illustrated in Fig. 7 . Moreover, microwave-assisted wet torrefaction at the power ranging from 600 to 800 W for 20 min in glycerol prior to liquefaction was found to be beneficial especially for SB and EFB, to improve the energy ratio of the bio-crude product. Energy ratios in a range of 2.0–2.7 obtained from bio-oil derived from microwave-assisted torrefaction followed by solvothermolysis liquefaction with glycerol were in similar ranges with bio-oil derived from sludge-to-oil reactor system process and microalgae from Pacific Northwest National Laboratory, USA [47]. Interestingly, the energy yield of light bio-oil (YE of 0.75–0.85) was as considerably high as upgraded microalgae derived bio-oil after hydrodeoxygenation and hydrodenitrogenation as commercial Pd/C catalyst [47,49]. While non-catalytic pyrolysis of wheat straw provided about 67% energy yield (YE of 0.67) of bio-oil in the 500–600 °C pyrolysis temperature and catalytic pyrolysis using H-ZSM-5 zeolite bed gave lower energy yield down to 50–58% (YE of 0.50–0.58) [48]. In the case of hydrothermal liquefaction of aspen wood in glycerol at 400 °C and 30 MPa, energy ratio was reported in the range of 0.39–0.62 [35], and hydrothermal liquefaction of spent coffee grounds achieved YE at 0.73 when using water-to-feed stock mass ratio of 1:20 at 275 °C for 10 min [52]. However, it has been reported that energy consumption ratio, a ratio of energy required against the energy produced from biomass conversion, is highly sensitive to the initial moisture content of biomass and this value becomes increasingly favorable at low moisture levels. For wet algal biomass with 80% moisture content, a study discovered that hydrothermal liquefaction is more favorable (energy consumption ratio (ECR) 0.44–0.63) than pyrolysis (ECR 0.92–1.24) due to the higher energy required during water volatilization in the latter technique [50].

To confirm that the hydrothermolysis and solvothermolysis liquefaction of biomass is carbon neutral and environmentally friendly technique, carbon yield was the key indicator. Apart from promising energy yields, the present study demonstrated a comparable carbon yield (89.82%) from EFB solvothermolysis liquefaction at 600 W (microwave torrefaction) with a previous work (carbon yield of 92% bio-oil) from EFB hydrothermal liquefaction (300 °C, 10 min, 1 MPa) catalyzed by oxidized Fe powder [53]. Carbon yield from co-liquefaction of aspen wood and glycerol at 400 °C and 30 MPa was in the range of 42%–66% [35]. A study on hydrothermal liquefaction of microalgal Chlorella pyrenoidosa revealed that when the hydrothermal temperature was increased from 180 °C to 240 °C, carbon recovery in solid residue was rapidly reduced from 73.9% to 11.8%, and the highest carbon recovery for the crude bio-oil was 55.3% at 300 °C [51], and even lower carbon recovery was obtained from previous reports in the range of 20–40% [54]. Consequently, our study demonstrated the advantages of enhanced energy yield and carbon recovery in considerably higher than most of the reported literature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nanodots and nanoflakes from biomass liquefaction
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon nanoparticles were produced from hydrothermal and solvothermolysis liquefaction of EFB, as illustrated in Fig. 8 . Carbon nanodots were isolated from LBO by toluene extraction. From TEM analysis, the morphology of carbon dots from hydrothermal liquefaction in water and 10 wt% Na2CO3 based on biomass weight is illustrated in Fig. 8(A). Spherically shaped carbon nanoparticles with the average mean diameter of 49.5 nm was observed. The particle sizes were distributed in a range of 20–80 nm (Fig. 8(B)). It was reported that the mechanism of carbon dots synthesis via hydrothermal method is composed of three steps. First, the formation of chain-intermediate compounds from small organic molecule precursors by a condensation reaction such as radical reaction [55], Schiff base condensation [56], aldol condensation [57], and amidation [58]. Second, the aggregation or polymerization of intermediates into polymer-like carbon dots also known as carbon clusters, which occurs by various interactions such as covalent, non-covalent or Van der Waal [59]. Third, the carbonization of polymer dots at high temperature to generate the carbon-core nanomaterial [60].

Remarkably, carbon nanoflakes were detected for EFB solvothermolysis liquefaction in glycerol, as shown in Fig. 8(C). This could happen by the effect of relatively aggressive liquefaction at 300 °C, while most of the hemicellulose and partial lignin constituents are converted into soluble organic compounds, and the crystalline cellulose is partially carbonized. Spontaneously, glycerol melt makes the loose connection between the microfibers of EFB, causing full separation of plant cell wall layers. Concurrently, the layers are carbonized by an increased temperature to 300 °C and activated by the alkali salt, rendered to their thickness reduction and micro- and mesoporosity generation. A similar finding was reported for carbon nanosheets synthesis from hemp using the hydrothermal technique in a combination with alkaline hydroxide activation [61]. The particle sizes of carbon nanoflakes were ranged from 30 to 190 nm with an average diameter of 100.4 nm (Fig. 8(D)). Similar size of carbon dots and carbon nanoflakes from this study is comparable to previous work. The carbon dots from the hydrothermal carbonization of crystalline cellulose [62] and carboxymethyl cellulose [63] have larger average sizes of 100 and 30.2 nm, respectively. The size differences can be attributed to the post-synthesis purification method. Yan et al. [62], applied high-speed centrifugation of carbon nanoparticles while Wu et al. [63], further dialyzed carbon dots after centrifugation lowering the average size and polydispersity of the carbon nanodots. The variation of feedstock materials, reaction temperature and time, and passivation technique have been reported to affect the particle size and photoluminescence properties [64,65].

This work demonstrated that Na2CO3 was a superior catalyst for hydrothermal liquefaction of SL, SB, EFB, and PF to produce light bio-oil. Among all biomass tested, SB and EFB were the most promising raw material for bio-oil production by solvothermolysis liquefaction in terms of bio-oil yield and energy ratio. Besides, solvothermolysis liquefaction in glycerol provided bio-oil with an extremely higher energy ratio and energy yield compared with hydrothermal liquefaction. The additional step of microwave-assisted wet-torrefaction to treat raw biomass is necessary for noticeably enhanced energy ratio and energy yield. Moreover, carbon was efficiently recovered in the bio-oil using microwave-assisted torrefaction pretreatment. However, the overall mass, energy, and carbon balance of all heavy, light bio-oil and gaseous products are essential to get the complete scenario and better assessment among all liquefaction processes. The structural elucidation and valorization of heavy bio-oil, are further state of study. Carbon nanodots and nanoflakes from hydrothermal and solvothermolysis liquefaction of biomass require further characterization for their optical and optoelectronic properties for additional applications.
